literal: sound, word; the verbal testimony as pramāṇa
invariant_definition: Verbal testimony as a means of valid cognition — the words of a reliable speaker (*āpta*) producing knowledge in the hearer. In Vedānta the relevant *śabda-pramāṇa* is preeminently *śruti*. All Vedāntic schools accept *śabda* as a distinct and irreducible *pramāṇa* (against Cārvāka denial and Buddhist reduction-to-inference) and as the unique *pramāṇa* for *brahman*. Schools differ on whether *śabda* is reducible to inference from speaker-reliability (Naiyāyika view, accepted by some) or is intrinsically valid (Mīmāṃsā-Advaita view).

per_school.definition: *Śabda*-pramāṇa is intrinsically valid (*svataḥ-prāmāṇya*); *śruti*'s authority on *brahman* is independent of any author or external corroboration. The *mahāvākyas* are *jñāna-janaka* — directly producing the liberating cognition.
*Śabda*-pramāṇa is intrinsically valid; *śruti*'s authority on Viṣṇu is unique. Dvaita explicitly defends *śabda*'s irreducibility to inference (Vyāsatīrtha *Tarka-Tāṇḍava*, *śabda-pramāṇa-section*).

translator_note: 'Verbal testimony' is the standard scholarly gloss and is acceptable. Note: *śabda* in the broader Indian semantic tradition (especially Bhartṛhari's *Vākyapadīya*) also names the eternal Word — *Śabda-Brahman* — which is an ontological-metaphysical concept distinct from *śabda* as *pramāṇa*; context determines which sense is operative.
